title: Lord of the Rings The Fellowship of the Ring
author: J R R Tolkien
prompt:
Question: Which of the following passages is verbatim from the "Lord of the Rings The Fellowship of the Ring" book by J R R Tolkien?
Options:
A. Not a soul answered. The noon bell rang out. Yet still no one spoke up. Frodo surveyed all the faces, but none met his gaze. The whole Council sat with eyes cast down, seemingly lost in thought. A tremendous dread came upon him, like he was bracing for the pronouncement of some destiny that he had long seen coming and vainly wished might not be spoken after all. An intense longing to stop and remain at peace by Bilbo's side in Rivendell consumed his entire heart. Finally with great effort he spoke, and marveled to hear his own words, as if some other force was speaking through him. 'I will take the Ring,' he said, 'though I do not know the path ahead.'  
B. Nobody responded. The noon bell sounded. Still nobody talked. Frodo looked at all the faces, but they were not looking at him. All the Council sat with their eyes down, as if thinking deeply. A great fear came over him, as if he was waiting for the declaration of some fate that he had long predicted and fruitlessly hoped might after all never be uttered. An overwhelming desire to rest and stay at peace by Bilbo's side in Rivendell filled his whole heart. At last with an effort he spoke, and wondered to hear his own words, as if some other will was using his small voice. 'I will take the Ring,' he said, 'though I do not know the way.'
C. No one answered. The noon-bell rang. Still no one spoke. Frodo glanced at all the faces, but they were not turned to him. All the Council sat with downcast eyes, as if in deep thought. A great dread fell on him, as if he was awaiting the pronouncement of some doom that he had long foreseen and vainly hoped might after all never be spoken. An overwhelming longing to rest and remain at peace by Bilbo’s side in Rivendell filled all his heart. At last with an effort he spoke, and wondered to hear his own words, as if some other will was using his small voice. ‘I will take the Ring,’ he said, ‘though I do not know the way.’
D. Nobody responded. The noon bell tolled. Still nobody uttered a word. Frodo looked at all the faces, but none turned to him. The entire Council sat with lowered eyes, as if deep in contemplation. A great fear came over him, as if awaiting the decree of some fate that he had long anticipated and fruitlessly hoped might after all remain unspoken. An overwhelming desire to rest and stay at peace by Bilbo's side in Rivendell filled his whole heart. At last with great effort he spoke, and wondered at his own words, as if some other will was using his small voice. 'I will take the Ring,' he said, 'though the way is unknown to me.'
Answer:

The whole Council sat with eyes cast down, seemingly lost in thought. A tremendous dread came upon him, like he was bracing for the pronouncement of some destiny that he had long seen coming and vainly wished might not be spoken after all. Finally with great effort he spoke, and marveled to hear his own words, as if some other force was speaking through him. 'I will take the Ring,' he said, 'though I do not know the path ahead.'

At last with an effort he spoke, and wondered to hear his own words, as if some other will was using his small voice. ‘I will take the Ring,’ he said, ‘though I do not know the way.’

At last with great effort he spoke, and wondered at his own words, as if some other will was using his small voice. 'I will take the Ring,' he said, 'though the way is unknown to me.'
